questions.text: What is the primary purpose of CSS3?

choices.label: A
choices.text: To define the structure of the document.

choices.label: B
choices.text: To specify the presentation of elements separately from the structure.

questions.explanation: CSS is used to separate the presentation (style) of a web document from its structural content (HTML).

questions.text: Which attribute is used to declare an **inline style** for an individual element?
questions.explanation: The `style` attribute is used in HTML to apply styles directly to specific elements.

questions.text: When declaring CSS properties, what character follows the property name and what character separates multiple declarations?
questions.explanation: In CSS, a colon (:) follows the property name (e.g., `color: red`), and a semicolon (;) separates individual declarations.

questions.text: Where are **embedded style sheets** typically placed within an HTML5 document?
questions.explanation: Embedded style sheets are placed within the `<head>` section of an HTML document using the `<style>` element.

questions.text: What is the correct MIME type for CSS style sheets?
questions.explanation: Style sheets use the `text/css` MIME type to specify the encoding format.

questions.text: In a CSS rule body, which symbols are used to enclose the declarations?
questions.explanation: The declarations of a CSS rule are enclosed within curly braces `{ }`.

questions.text: In the `font-weight` property, the value `normal` is numerically equivalent to which value?
questions.explanation: Text specified as `normal` is equivalent to the numeric weight of 400, while `bold` is equivalent to 700.

questions.text: How is a **style class** selector represented in a CSS style sheet?
questions.explanation: Class selectors in CSS are preceded by a period (e.g., `.myClass`).

questions.text: How do you define a selector for an element with a specific `id`?
questions.explanation: ID selectors are defined using the hash (`#`) symbol followed by the specific `id` value of the element.

questions.text: Why are relative `font-size` values (like `em` or `%`) generally preferred over absolute values (like `pt`) for web content?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Relative values are easier for developers to calculate.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Absolute values are deprecated in CSS3.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Relative values allow users to adjust text size in their browser for better accessibility.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Relative values ensure the font is always the same physical size across different hardware.

questions.explanation: Relative units permit more flexible viewing and allow users to scale text based on their specific display settings and accessibility needs.

questions.text: According to the cascading rules, which of the following has the highest precedence for normal declarations?
questions.explanation: In the CSS cascade, for normal (non-important) declarations, author styles take precedence over user styles, which in turn take precedence over user agent (default browser) styles.

questions.text: If a child element and a parent element have conflicting CSS properties (where the child would otherwise inherit the value), which one takes precedence?

choices.label: B
choices.text: The child element's property because a direct rule overrides inheritance.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The properties are merged and both are applied simultaneously.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The conflict remains unresolved and the browser ignores both styles.

questions.explanation: Properties defined directly for an element always take precedence over properties inherited from an ancestor element.

questions.text: Which `text-decoration` value is used to apply a strike-through effect to text?
questions.explanation: The `line-through` value of the `text-decoration` property draws a horizontal line through the center of the text.

questions.text: Which pseudo-class is activated when the user moves the mouse cursor over an element?
questions.explanation: The `:hover` pseudo-class applies when the user designates an element with a pointing device, such as a mouse cursor.

questions.text: Which of the following is considered an absolute-length measurement unit in CSS?
questions.explanation: Points (`pt`) are absolute units defined as 1/72 of an inch. While `px` is also technically an absolute unit in modern CSS specs, units like `em`, `rem`, and `ex` are relative.

questions.text: In CSS, what is the relative unit `em` based on?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The height of a font's lowercase 'x'.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The computed font-size of the element.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The default font size of the browser's root element.

questions.explanation: In CSS, `1em` is equal to the computed font-size of the element on which it is used. When used on the `font-size` property itself, it is relative to the parent's font-size.

questions.text: What is the primary benefit of using external style sheets?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They increase the file size of individual HTML documents.

choices.label: B
choices.text: They allow for site-wide style changes by modifying a single CSS file.

choices.label: C
choices.text: They are the only method available to apply CSS to HTML.

choices.label: D
choices.text: They ensure that styles are never cached by the browser.

questions.explanation: External style sheets decouple content from presentation, allowing a single file to define the look and feel of an entire website.

questions.text: What are the correct symbols for starting and ending a CSS comment?
questions.explanation: CSS uses C-style multi-line comments starting with `/*` and ending with `*/`. `<!-- -->` is for HTML, and `#` is used for IDs or in other languages like Python.

questions.text: Which attribute of the `<link>` element specifies the relationship between the HTML document and the external file?
questions.explanation: The `rel` attribute defines the relationship. For a stylesheet, the syntax is `<link rel="stylesheet" href="styles.css">`.

questions.text: What does the `z-index` property control in CSS?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The horizontal (X-axis) position of an element.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The vertical (Y-axis) position of an element.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The stacking order (Z-axis) of overlapping positioned elements.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The magnification or zoom level of an element.

questions.explanation: The `z-index` property specifies the stack order of an element. An element with a greater stack order is always in front of an element with a lower stack order.

questions.text: When an element is positioned **absolutely**, it is removed from the normal flow and positioned relative to:

choices.label: C
choices.text: The very center of the screen only.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The next element in the document.

questions.explanation: Absolute positioning moves an element relative to its nearest positioned ancestor (an element with a position other than `static`). If no such ancestor exists, it is positioned relative to the initial containing block (usually the viewport).

questions.text: Which of the following is an example of an **inline-level element**?
questions.explanation:
``, ``, and `` are inline-level elements. `

`, `

`, and `

` are block-level elements.

questions.text: What is a characteristic of **block-level elements**?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They do not start on a new line.

choices.label: B
choices.text: They are displayed on their own line and occupy the full width available.

choices.label: D
choices.text: They are used only for text formatting like bold or italic.

questions.explanation: Block-level elements begin on a new line and occupy the full width available, acting as 'boxes' in the page layout.

questions.text: How do you specify the location of a background image in CSS?
questions.explanation: The correct syntax for applying a background image is `background-image: url('fileLocation');`.

questions.text: What is the default behavior of the `background-repeat` property?
questions.explanation: The default value for `background-repeat` is `repeat`, which tiles the image both vertically and horizontally to fill the element.

questions.text: To keep a background image from moving while the user scrolls the page, which property and value should be used?
questions.explanation: Setting `background-attachment: fixed;` keeps the background image stationary relative to the viewport while the content scrolls.

questions.text: Which property is used to indent the first line of text in an element?
questions.explanation: The `text-indent` property is used to specify the indentation of the first line of text in a block-level element.

questions.text: If an element's content is too large for its specified dimensions, which property can add scroll bars?
questions.explanation: The `overflow` property controls what happens to content that is too big to fit into an area. Setting it to `scroll` or `auto` adds scroll bars.

questions.text: In the CSS Box Model, what is the correct order of layers from the center outwards?
questions.explanation: The box model consists of the Content at the center, followed by Padding, then the Border, and finally the Margin on the outermost layer.

questions.text: Which property determines the distance between the content inside an element and the edge of the element (the inside of the border)?
questions.explanation: Padding is the space between the content of the element and its border. Margin is the space outside the border.

questions.text: Which property determines the distance between an element's edge (the border) and any outside text or adjacent elements?
questions.explanation: The `margin` property is used to create space around elements, outside of any defined borders.

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT a valid value for the `border-style` property?
questions.explanation: Valid CSS `border-style` values include `dotted`, `dashed`, `solid`, `double`, `groove`, `ridge`, `inset`, and `outset`. There is no standard `striped` value.

questions.text: What is the primary purpose of the `float` property in CSS?

choices.label: A
choices.text: To make an element hover in 3D space.

choices.label: B
choices.text: To move an element to one side of its container, allowing text and inline elements to wrap around it.

choices.label: C
choices.text: To increase the `z-index` of an element automatically.

choices.label: D
choices.text: To adjust the transparency of an element.

questions.explanation: The `float` property places an element on the left or right side of its container, allowing other content to flow around it.

questions.text: Which CSS media type is used specifically for standard computer screens?
questions.explanation: The `screen` media type is intended for color computer screens.

questions.text: How do you define a block of styles that applies only when a document is being printed?
questions.explanation: The `@media print` rule is a media query used to apply CSS specifically for printed materials and print previews.

questions.text: In CSS media queries, which orientation is applied if the device's screen width is greater than its height?
questions.explanation: The `orientation: landscape` media feature applies when the width of the viewport is greater than its height.

questions.text: Which property and value should be used to hide an element so that it is not rendered and takes up no space in the layout?
questions.explanation: Unlike `visibility: hidden`, which leaves a blank space where the element would be, `display: none` removes the element from the document flow entirely.

questions.text: Where are User Style Sheets typically defined?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Linked in the `<head>` of the HTML document.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Within the browser's own settings or local user configuration files.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hard-coded into the operating system kernel.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Provided by the web server via a backend database.

questions.explanation: User style sheets are local CSS files or settings provided by the user in their browser to override or augment the styles of the websites they visit.

questions.text: Which type of font size unit is best for accessibility because it scales based on user-defined browser settings?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Percentage units relative only to the container width

questions.explanation: Relative units like `em` and `rem` allow text to scale according to the user's browser preferences, whereas absolute units can be difficult for browsers to override.

questions.text: What is the primary purpose of using a CSS validator?

choices.label: A
choices.text: To check if the website is hosted on a high-performance server.

choices.label: B
choices.text: To verify that the CSS code is syntactically correct and adheres to W3C standards.

choices.label: C
choices.text: To automatically convert HTML structure into CSS rules.

choices.label: D
choices.text: To scan the style sheet for potential malware or viruses.

questions.explanation: A CSS validator checks your code for syntax errors and ensures it follows the specifications set by the W3C.

questions.text: Which generic font family would be a safe fallback if a specific font like 'Times New Roman' is not available?
questions.explanation: The `serif` generic font family is the standard fallback for fonts with small decorative lines at the ends of characters, such as 'Times New Roman'.

questions.text: The CSS property `font-style` is primarily used to set text to which of the following?
questions.explanation: The `font-style` property is used to specify whether text should be rendered in `normal`, `italic`, or `oblique` styles.

questions.text: Which property allows you to center text within a block-level element?
questions.explanation: The `text-align` property with a value of `center` is used to align the inline content (like text) within its block-level container.

questions.text: If you want to apply a purple color to multiple unrelated elements across your page, which selector type is most appropriate?
questions.explanation: Class selectors are intended for reusable styles that can be applied to any number of elements, whereas ID selectors should be unique to a single element.

questions.text: What is the result of applying `position: relative;` to an element?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The element is positioned relative to its normal position in the document flow.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The element is fixed to the top-left of the viewport.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The element is removed from the document flow and positioned relative to the page.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The element behaves exactly like a block-level element with no ability to offset it.

questions.explanation: Relative positioning allows you to offset an element using `top`, `right`, `bottom`, or `left` without removing it from the natural document flow.

questions.text: In the context of the CSS box model, which property can take keyword values like `thin`, `medium`, or `thick`?
questions.explanation: The `border-width` property sets the thickness of an element's border and accepts specific length units or the keywords `thin`, `medium`, and `thick`.

questions.text: Which media feature would you use in a media query to check the aspect ratio of the output device's screen?
questions.explanation: The `device-aspect-ratio` media feature is used to query the width-to-height ratio of the entire output device's screen.

questions.text: What is the relationship between the keyword `bold` and the numerical `font-weight` scale?
questions.explanation: In the standard `font-weight` scale, `normal` corresponds to `400` and `bold` corresponds to `700`.

questions.text: Which element is used as a generic block-level grouping element?
questions.explanation: The `<div>` element is a generic block-level container, whereas `<span>` is a generic inline-level container.

questions.text: Which measurement unit is based on the height of a font's lowercase 'x'?
questions.explanation: The `ex` unit is a relative length unit that corresponds to the x-height of the current font (roughly the height of the lowercase 'x').
